title: Efficiency Calculator – Energy, Power & System Efficiency
tagline: Calculate efficiency ratios, energy losses, and power conversion rates for motors, electrical systems, heat engines, and mechanical devices.
intro: Enter input and output energy or power values, select your system type, and get the efficiency percentage and energy loss instantly.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for at least input and output energy or input and output power.
error.zeroInput: Input energy and input power cannot be zero.

about.heading: About the Efficiency Calculator
about.body:
Efficiency is one of the most fundamental concepts in physics and engineering. In its most general form, efficiency is the ratio of useful output to total input, expressed as a percentage. When you put 1000 joules of energy into a motor and get 850 joules of mechanical work out, the motor is 85% efficient—the remaining 150 joules are lost, primarily as heat due to friction, resistance, and other dissipative mechanisms. This calculator handles the energy form (η = E_out / E_in × 100%) and the power form (η = P_out / P_in × 100%) simultaneously, since both give identical results when measured over the same time interval.

Efficiency appears in every branch of science and engineering. Electrical engineers rate motors, transformers, and power supplies by their conversion efficiency to predict heat generation and calculate operating costs. Mechanical engineers analyse gearboxes, pumps, and turbines to minimise friction losses. Thermodynamicists compare real heat engines against the theoretical Carnot efficiency ceiling, which is determined by the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs. HVAC engineers use Coefficient of Performance (COP)—a related concept—to quantify how many joules of heat a heat pump moves per joule of electricity consumed, yielding values that can exceed 100% in apparent terms because energy is being moved rather than converted.

The Second Law of Thermodynamics guarantees that no real conversion process can be 100% efficient. Every time energy changes form—chemical to thermal in combustion, thermal to mechanical in a turbine, mechanical to electrical in a generator, electrical to mechanical in a motor—some fraction is irreversibly dispersed into low-grade heat. Understanding and minimising these losses is one of the core tasks of sustainable engineering.

In practice, efficiency depends on operating conditions. Most motors and engines have a characteristic efficiency curve that peaks at a specific load. Running a motor at 10% of rated load or 200% of rated load both degrade efficiency. Variable-frequency drives (VFDs) for motors and variable-speed compressors in HVAC systems exploit this by keeping the equipment in its high-efficiency operating band. Measuring efficiency at several operating points, not just at rated conditions, gives a far more accurate picture of system performance over a typical duty cycle.

Energy audits routinely use efficiency analysis as a diagnostic tool. By comparing the efficiency of a system under test to its nameplate or design value, engineers can detect wear, contamination, or component failure long before catastrophic breakdown occurs. A pump whose efficiency has dropped from 82% to 74% is still functioning, but it is consuming 11% more electricity for the same flow—a detectable economic signal that justifies maintenance. This calculator provides the arithmetic backbone for such comparisons in a fast, clear format.

examples.heading: Efficiency Calculator Examples
examples.intro: Three real-world scenarios showing how to enter data and interpret the efficiency results.

items.input: Electric motor: input 5000 J, output 4250 J, input power 1000 W, output power 850 W
items.note: Standard industrial motor. Energy loss = 750 J; power loss = 150 W. Consistent energy and power efficiencies confirm steady-state operation.

items.input: Steam turbine: input 10 000 J, output 3500 J, input power 2000 W, output power 700 W
items.note: Typical thermal power-plant heat engine. Carnot limit for 600 °C/30 °C steam is ~66%; real turbines approach 40–45% at best.

items.input: LED light: input power 10 W, output useful light power 8 W
items.note: Modern LEDs convert ~80% of electrical input to visible light, versus ~5% for incandescent bulbs, demonstrating huge efficiency gains in solid-state lighting.

items.input: Hydraulic pump: input energy 2000 J, output hydraulic energy 1640 J
items.note: Typical volumetric and mechanical efficiency combined. The 360 J loss heats the hydraulic oil, requiring proper thermal management of the reservoir.

howto.heading: How to Use the Efficiency Calculator
steps: Enter the input energy in joules and the output (useful) energy in joules. For power-based analysis, also enter the input power in watts and output power in watts.
Select the system type from the dropdown if it applies — this provides context for interpreting the result but does not change the calculation.
Optionally enter the operating time in hours. This is used to cross-check energy and power values but does not affect the efficiency ratio.
Click Calculate. The calculator displays energy efficiency, power efficiency, energy loss, and power loss in a single summary.
Compare the result against the manufacturer's nameplate efficiency or design specification. Any shortfall indicates losses from wear, fouling, or suboptimal operating conditions.

items.q: What is the formula for efficiency?
items.a: Efficiency η = (useful output / total input) × 100%. For energy: η = (E_out / E_in) × 100%. For power: η = (P_out / P_in) × 100%. Both ratios give the same result when the system operates at steady state over the same time interval.

For energy-conversion devices (motors, engines, turbines), efficiency cannot exceed 100% — that would violate the First Law of Thermodynamics. However, heat pumps and refrigerators appear to exceed 100% because they move heat rather than convert it; their Coefficient of Performance (COP) can be 2–5 or higher.

items.q: What is the difference between energy efficiency and power efficiency?
items.a: They measure the same underlying ratio from different perspectives. Energy efficiency compares total joules in versus total joules of useful output over a fixed period. Power efficiency compares instantaneous watts in versus watts of useful output. At steady state they are identical; they diverge during start-up and shut-down transients.

items.q: Why is no real machine 100% efficient?
items.a: The Second Law of Thermodynamics states that every real energy conversion generates entropy, which manifests as irreversible heat loss to the environment. Friction, electrical resistance, fluid turbulence, magnetic hysteresis, and leakage are examples. The Carnot efficiency sets a theoretical upper limit for heat engines based solely on source and sink temperatures.

items.q: How do I improve a system's efficiency?
items.a: Identify the dominant loss mechanism first. For motors, reducing winding resistance (copper losses) or improving magnetic materials (iron losses) helps. For heat engines, increasing operating temperature or reducing waste-heat temperature improves the Carnot limit. Matching the load to the design operating point is often the single highest-impact change.

items.q: What is a good efficiency for common systems?
items.a: LED lights achieve 80–90% lighting efficiency; electric motors 85–97%; large steam turbines 35–45%; internal combustion engines 20–40%; transformers 95–99%; switching power supplies 85–95%. These benchmarks help you judge whether a measured efficiency indicates healthy operation or hidden losses requiring attention.
